EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large, and rather moist storm system moved up from the southwest from the tropics. This storm system dropped heavy snow to northern and central Idaho before raising snow levels to over 7000 feet by the evening of the 18th. Across the Northern Panhandle, 6 inches was reported by a spotter at Bonners Ferry while just down the road 12 miles a spotter reported 10 inches of new snow. About 8 inches fell at the town of Sandpoint. Over the Coeur D'Alene Area, 3 to 4 inches of snow was common but a spotter at the town of Rathdrum reported 6 inches of new snow. Over the Idaho Palouse, 3 to 5 inches of snow was common. Over the Central Panhandle Mountains, 9 inches fell over Silver Mountain and 8-12 inches were common over all other mountain locations. Two Snowboarders died in an avalance just south of the town of Mullan on the afternoon of the 16th.

A large pacific storm system brought heavy snow to all of northern and central Idaho. In the Northern Panhandle Mountains, 4 inches were common in the valley locations with spotters reporting 4 inches at Sandpoint and 4 inches at Bonners Ferry. The mountains of the Northern Panhandle Mountains received 8 to 15 inches of new snow. In the Central Panhandle Mountains, 10 to 20 inches were common over the mountains while 4 inches were mostly reported over the valley locations. A spotter reported 4 inches at the town of St. Maries. In the Coeur D'Alene Area, spotters reported 4 inches at Spirit Lake and 6 inches at Rathdrum. The Idaho Palouse had around 4 to 5 inches with a spotter reporting 5 inches at Plummer and 4 inches at Moscow.

A  powerful winter storm rolled in off the Pacific and brought significant snow and winds to all of southeast Idaho beginning on the afternoon of the 7th and continuing into the morning of the 9th.  Heavy snow fell overnight on the 7th and morning of the 8th in the central mountains and Upper Snake River Highlands.  Very strong winds occurred overnight in the Southern Highlands and Snake River Plain with sustained winds of 40 to 50 mph with gusts of 60 to 70 mph common causing blowing and drifting snow.  The winds caused $3000 worth of damage to a TJ Maxx sign in Pocatello plus $6000 additional damage to buildings and roofs.  Heavier snow amounts through the morning of the 8th were 14 inches at Island Park, 20 inches at Ketchum, 12 inches at Galena, 18 inches at Arco, 10 inches at Mackay, 12 inches at Moore, 16 inches at Hailey, 12 inches at Macks Inn, 12 inches at Ashton, 12 inches at Driggs, 12 inches at Pomerelle Ski Area.  Several roads were closed on the 8th including Highway 32 from Ashton to Tetonia, US20 from Idaho Falls to Arco, and interstate 15 from mile post 167 to 196.  Heavy snow continued to spread southeast through the day on the 8th with amounts of 8 to 12 inches across the Snake River Plain including Burley, American Falls, Pocatello, Fort Hall, and Idaho Falls.  14 to 18 inches fell in the Caribou and Southern Highlands including Oakley, Soda Springs, and Grace.  The official snowfall of 8.3 inches on the 8th at the Pocatello airport was a calendar day record for the month of January in the city.  Heaviest snowfall totals from the two day event at SNOTEL sites above 7000 feet were 32 inches at Vienna Mine, 28 inches at Lost Wood Divide, 28 inches at Galena Summit, 34 inches at Dollarhide, 26 inches at Slug Creek, 36 inches at Sedgewick Peak, 32 inches at Bear Canyon, and 32 inches at Crab Creek.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong jet stream came across the Idaho Palouse during the evening hours of the 11th of January and continued into the evening hours of the 12th. This upper level jet brought high winds to the Idaho Palouse with numerous power outages being reported with gusts to around 60 mph. Some of the towns receiving power outages with the high winds were Moscow and Genesee.

A reinforcing shot of cold air followed the storm system that hit northern Idaho on the 6th through the 8th of January. This smaller system brought another round of heavy snow to the Idaho panhandle. In the northern panhandle, a spotter in Athol reported 5 inches of new snow. In the central panhandle, the mountains received 8 inches of new snow with both Lookout Pass and Silver Mountain reporting 8 inches. in the Coeur D'Alene Area, a spotter reported 5 inches around the town of Coeur D'Alene. The Idaho Palouse received 4 to 5 inches of new snow with a spotter in the town of Plummer reporting 5 inches.

The trailing cold front caused strong winds along the Snake River.

An arctic cold front pushed through western Montana and into north central Idaho, producing heavy snow and strong gusty winds. 24 hour total snowfall amounts ranged from 4 to 6 inches in the valleys of the northern Clearwater Mountains, up to one foot in the mountains. Wind gusts of 25 to 30 mph reduced visibility in blowing snow. In the southern Clearwater Mountains, 24 hour snowfall totals ranged from 8 inches in the valleys to 12 to 14 inches in the mountains. Wind gusts of 25 to 40 mph caused reduced visibility in blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early spring snowstorm brought heavy snow to the mountains or eastern Idaho.  13 to 14 inches fell at the Bear Canyon and Vienna Mine SNOTEL sites in Custer County.  11 inches fell at the Crab Creek SNOTEL site in Clark County.

A strong cold front moved through southeast Idaho during the afternoon and evening hours and high winds were felt in the Snake River Plain ahead of as well as behind the front.  Craters of the Moon reported one and a half hours of sustained 40 to 50 mph winds with gusts to 63 mph.  Richfield reported a sustained wind of 40 mph at 425 pm MST with gusts to 60 mph.  Raft River reported sustained winds of 40 to 45 mph from 455 pm MST to 635 pm MST.  Minidoka reported sustained 42 to 51 mph winds from 450 pm MST through 635 pm MST with gusts of 60 to 65 mph.  Two telephone poles in the city of Burley were blown over as well as a silo and a large spruce tree within the city.  Power lines were downed in Bingham County with power outages in Rockford and west of Springfield.  Power lines were down in Blackfoot and Chubbuck and Pocatello in Bannock County with minor power outages.  Power outages also reported in American Falls in Power County.

Wind damage reported in Bannock County.  Several fences blown down as well as a basketball stand broken in wind.

A spring snowstorm brought late season snow to the Snake River Plain and surrounding mountains.  3 to 5 inches were reported in Burley, American Falls and Pocatello with 4 to 6 inches in Mink Creek and Oakley.  Several minor traffic accidents were reported during the evening of the 19th in Burley but no injuries reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small storm system dropping down from western British Columbia moved across the Camas Prairie on the night of April 11 through the morning of April 12th. About 4 inches of snow were common across the higher portions of the Camas Prairie with 4.6 inches being reported at the town of Winchester.

On the evening of the 6th of May, a slow moving line of thunderstorms moved across central Idaho. These thunderstorms dumped one to three inches of rain in an hour across portions of the Idaho Palouse. A flash flood moved into Hubble Gulch and washed out Hubble Gulch Road and a few structures in Nez Perce County. Numerous roads were also washed out in Latah County with the same storms. There was an estimated 2 million dollars damage to roads and structures total for both counties because of the flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A slow moving thunderstorm moved 2 miles east of the town of Lapwai on the afternoon of the 8th of May. An estimated 2 to 3 inches fell across the headwaters of Garden Gulch Creek in about an hour. A flash flood then raced through Garden Gulch and flooded out structures, killed some livestock and washed out roads to the intersection of State Route 95 and Garden Gulch Road. An estimated 4 million dollars in property and roads was estimated in a five mile swath along Garden Gulch.
Several mesonet sites in central Butte County reported above 50 knot wind gusts.
Several trees downed in Chubbuck.  Power lines down with outages.  Damage to a trailer from a tree falling on it.
Several trees downed by winds in Fort Hall.
Tornado in Mud Lake area.  Several trees downed and one tractor moved across highway and rolled over.
Electrical power outage east of Howe.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the evening of the 9th of May, a storm system brought slow moving thunderstorms across portions of Central Idaho. These storms dumped 2 to 5 inches of rain in about 6 hours in eastern Latah County. There were numerous road closures due to the heavy rain and many homes were flooded in eastern Latah County. Many locations on Highways 8 and 95 around Moscow were closed and damaged due to debris flows.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Highway sign at exit blown into car windshield.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Did damage to two wheel-line irrigation systems and two center pivot irrigation systems.
Power lines blown down with power outages in Howe.
One inch hail in Richfield.
Tornado in open sagebrush area.  No damage noted.
Thunderstorm winds uprooted large trees in Pocatello.  One fell into a house causing extensive damage.
Marsh Creek in Albion flooded.  A culvert was washed and one home was flooded.

1 to 2 inches of rainfall in a 2 hour period created extensive flash flooding in the towns of Albion and Oakley.  In Albion, Marsh and Land Creeks flooded with the city of Albion declared a disaster area.  3 county road bridges/culverts were washed out.  This cut off access to 6 homes.  A foot of water was flowing across state highway 77 with damage to other county roads.  8 to 11 homes in Albion reported extensive basement and/or bedroom and kitchen flooding.  Birch Creek created flooding in Oakley.  Water from the Birch Creek Diversion canal flooded numerous roads but caused no damage.  1  home had basement flooding.
Tornado in open fields.  No damage reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred along the Little Wood River near Carey.  The river reached 7.1 feet at Carey with a flood stage of 6.0 feet.  The river peaked on May 17th  at 7 am and was below flood stage by May 18th at 9 am...dropping to flood stage at around midnight.  All damage was south of Carey.  One home had water in the lower level.  5 homes were surrounded, and there was one minor washout on a state highway bridge which was quickly repaired.
Thunderstorm winds blew down 3 road signs between Chubbuck and Fort Hall.
Lightning in Burley struck an electrical light system at Overland and 14th Street causing damage.
Tornado touching ground east of Stone over mountains.
Several mesonet sites at the Idaho National Engineering Laboratory reported wind gusts over 50 knots in Butte County.
59 mph wind gust at Bald Mountain.

A strong thunderstorm brought wind damage to much of Cassia County.  Several trees were downed with at least 7 vehicles damaged by falling trees in Oakley.  Sate highway 77 in Albion was blocked by a downed tree for more than an hour.  In Declo, a tree crashed through the roof of a house on Main Street.  Several power outages also were reported.

Southern Minidoka County was hit by severe thunderstorms.  The Minidoka mesonet site recorded a wind gust of 72 mph.  Several residents of Rupert were without power and two falling trees caused damage to a home on Third Street.  Several downed trees were reported in Rupert.
Rover mesonet site.
Several mesonet sites at the Idaho National Engineering Laboratory recorded wind gusts over 58 mph with the highest recorded being 75 mph at 1830 MDT at the Waste Management Site.

Many large tree limbs twisted off and/or broken.  Empty gas tank at the airport blown off stand.  Power poles and lines downed.
Thunderstorm winds blew down several power lines in Hailey.
68  mph wind at Bald Mountain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms with damaging winds moved through eastern Lemhi County during the evening hours. Wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph were reported by Sheriff Dispatch and trained spotters. Sheriff reported one house with a roof blown off and numerous power lines down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wildfire destroyed 20 acres of a grain field in the Michaud Creek area of the Arbon Valley east of American Falls.  Fire contained after an hour.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the evening of the 21st, a severe thunderstorm gust front moved across Kootenai County at around 715pm to about 815pm PST. The hardest hit towns were Post Falls and Rathdrum. In the town of Post Falls, many large trees were down along with about 10,000 homes without power. There were a few homes damaged by the winds. The town of Rathdrum had about 200 homes without power and trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong summer storm brought both severe winds and hail to Northwest Idaho County. Bulk of damage occurred from Grangeville to Fenn.  Four power poles were knocked down with hail reported as large as 1.25 inches. Wind gusts measured to 70 mph caused a roof  to be ripped off a barn, which then collapsed onto farm equipment. The roof was also ripped off of the Grangeville Public Works building. Extensive crop damage was also reported on the Camas Prairie.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Canadian cold front moved across south central Idaho during the early evening hours of the 12th of August. A severe thunderstorm brought high wind gusts to the town of Lewiston and downed a few trees and caused damage to a few roofs. Damage estimates were on the order of twenty thousand dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stubble and brush fire burned 12 acres in Fort Hall.  Four homes were evacuated.  No damage occurred.

A localized winter storm brought heavy snow to the Pocatello area.  Snowfall amounts increased rapidly from west to east with amounts of around 4 inches at the NWS office at the airport increasing to 6 to 8 inches in Chubbuck with amounts of 10 to 14 inches in downtown Pocatello and the benches east of the city.  A slow moving upper trof combined with localized convergence contributed to the heavy snowfall in Pocatello.  Snowfall amounts decreased rapidly as you moved away from Pocatello.  Wind speeds of 20 to 30 mph contributed extensive blowing and drifting snow as well.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A pacific storm system moved across extreme northern Idaho during the afternoon hours of the 5th and into the morning of the 6th. Snow accumulations of 4 to 6 inches were common across the northern Idaho Panhandle with Eastport reporting 4 inches. Schwitzer Mountain reported 9 inches of new snow during this event.

A winter storm moved through the early morning hours of the 12th and into the afternoon of the 13th. Snow amounts of 4 to 7 inches were common with Hidden Lake receiving 6 inches while Schweitzer Mountain received 10 inches of new snow.

During the night of December 1st and into the early morning hours of the 2nd, a warm front moved across portions of northern Idaho. The warm front brought a mixture of light precipitation to the Coeur D'Alene area and caused over two dozen auto accidents. All of the auto accidents were minor with no injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Water backing up behind an ice jam on the upper portion of Orofino Creek broke and caused a flash flood event to occur along the banks of Orofino Creek. 3 homes and a barn along the creek were impacted by the flood waters. One home had flood damage in the basement.

Several fires made up the Fort Hall Complex believed to have been started by dry lightning early in the morning on September 4th.   The fires were comprised fo the Rattlesnake fire in Arbon Valley.  The Sawmill and Putnam fires east of Fort Hall as well as the Yandell fire northeast of Fort Hall.  The Rattlesnake Fire forced the evacuation of three homes and caused closures on Arbon Valley Road, Trail Creek Road and Simplot Road on September 4th.  The fire burned about 20,000 acres including 6,120 acres of Fort Hall Indian Reservation land,   2,218 acres of BLM land, 1,831 acres of private land, and 549 acres of Forest Service Land.  The fort Hall Complex was considered 100 percent contained by September 12th at an estimated cost of 3.3 million dollars.
Dry lightning was believed to have started the Juniper wildfire east of Bridge near the Utah/Idaho border.  The fire burned 3,000 acres of mainly juniper and was in a remote location.
The Big Dry Canyon wildfire burned about 12,000 acres of remote land east of Swan Valley and south of Victor in northeast Bonneville.  the fire was contained by September 18th.

Gusty south winds were recorded over the Southern Clearwater Mountains and over higher terrain in Lemhi County where wind gusts of 30 to 35 mph caused areas of blowing and drifting snow.

Several fires made up the Fort Hall Complex believed to have been started by dry lightning early in the morning on September 4th.   The fires were comprised fo the Rattlesnake fire in Arbon Valley.  The Sawmill and Putnam fires east of Fort Hall as well as the Yandell fire northeast of Fort Hall.  The Rattlesnake Fire forced the evacuation of three homes and caused closures on Arbon Valley Road, Trail Creek Road and Simplot Road on September 4th.  The fire burned about 20,000 acres including 6,120 acres of Fort Hall Indian Reservation land,   2,218 acres of BLM land, 1,831 acres of private land, and 549 acres of Forest Service Land.  The fort Hall Complex was considered 100 percent contained by September 12th at an estimated cost of 3.3 million dollars.

Oak Street and 6th and 8th Avenues flooded as well.  One house on Oak Street reported basement flooding.  Minor flooding was reported in parts of the Arbon Valley in southeast Power county as well.
